Cardillo Documentation
  • Solvers
  • QOCO
  • View page source

QOCO

QOCO is an interior-point second-order cone program (SOCP) solver. Instead of iterating projected impulses, it finds the exact solution to the contact and constraint problem in one interior-point solve by treating contact feasibility as a cone constraint in a convex program.

QOCO is loaded dynamically at runtime and can run on CPU or CUDA depending on which backend library is available.

The problem

The full step is cast as a convex quadratic program with cone constraints:

\[\begin{split}\min_{x} \quad &\tfrac{1}{2} x^T P x + c^T x \\ \text{subject to} \quad &Ax = b \\ &Gx \in \mathcal{K}\end{split}\]

where:

  • \(x = (v,\ \lambda_g,\ \lambda_\gamma)\) is the combined velocity and multiplier vector

  • \(P\) is block-diagonal with the mass matrix, spring compliance, and damper compliance scaled by the time step

  • \(c\) encodes the negative momentum and external force contribution

  • \(A\), \(b\) enforce the constraint equations (spring and damper rows)

  • \(G\), \(h\) with cone \(\mathcal{K}\) enforce contact conditions

The cone \(\mathcal{K}\) is a product of:

  • a non-negative orthant for frictionless contacts (normal impulse >= 0)

  • one second-order cone of size 3 per frictional contact (Coulomb disk: tangential norm <= mu * normal)

This formulation gives an exact Coulomb friction cone rather than a linearised approximation.

How it solves

QOCO uses a primal-dual interior-point method. At each interior-point iteration:

  1. Compute the search direction by solving the KKT system.

  2. Apply KKT regularization (static and dynamic) for numerical stability.

  3. Take a step that keeps all cone variables strictly inside the cone.

  4. Check primal and dual residuals and duality gap for convergence.

Because the problem is convex, the interior-point method finds the global optimum without any projection heuristics.

Backend selection

QOCO is loaded at runtime via dlopen. The backend is selected by qoco.backend:

  • auto (default) - tries CUDA first, falls back to CPU

  • cpu - uses the CPU library

  • cuda - uses the CUDA-accelerated library

The library paths are set at build time via CMake.

Limitations

  • The solver is re-initialised every step because the contact sparsity pattern changes when contacts appear or disappear. There is no incremental update path.

  • Implicit gyroscopy (moreau.implicit_gyroscopy) and lambda-theta integration (moreau.lambda_theta) are not supported; these config options are ignored with a warning.

  • Warmstart stores previous contact impulses in the collision manager, but QOCO itself does not use them as an initial iterate (it starts from scratch each step).

Config keys

Key

Effect

qoco.backend

auto / cpu / cuda

solver.max_iterations

Maximum interior-point iterations

solver.tol_abs, solver.tol_rel

Primal/dual feasibility and gap tolerances

solver.kkt_static_reg

Static KKT regularisation constant

solver.kkt_dynamic_reg

Dynamic KKT regularisation delta
